analogy: Think of "Rise of Popular Movements" through the chapter's own opening images: In this chapter… We have seen in the last chapter that the Congress recovered after 1971, but was not the same kind of party. The difference became clear in a series of events between 1973 and 1975 that brought new challenges to India’s democratic politics... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 94 Politics in India since Independence, 96 Politics in India since Independence, 98 Politics in India since Independence, 11 December 1975. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Rise of Popular Movements is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that in this chapter… We have seen in the last chapter that the Congress recovered after 1971, but was not the same kind of party.

The text explains that the difference became clear in a series of events between 1973 and 1975 that brought new challenges to India’s democratic politics and the institutional balance sought by the Constitution.

The text explains that these developments led to the imposition of ‘emergency’ in June 1975.

The text explains that normally, we would associate ‘emergency’ with war and aggression or with natural disaster.

The text explains that but this ‘emergency’ was imposed because of the perceived threat of internal disturbance.

The text explains that the Emergency ended as dramatically as it had begun, resulting in a defeat of the Congress in the Lok Sabha elections of 1977.

The text explains that in this chapter we focus on this crucial phase in the history of democracy in India and ask some questions that have remained controversial after all these years. • Why was Emergency imposed?

The text explains that was it necessary? • What did the imposition of Emergency mean in practice? • What were the consequences of Emergency on party politics? • What are the lessons of Emergency for Indian democracy?

The text explains that the editorial page of ‘Nai Dunia’ of 27 June 1975 was like any other day, except that the space for editorial was left blank.

The text explains that the editorial was “censored” using emergency powers.

The text explains that many other newspapers also carried such blank spaces–sometimes to protest against emergency.

The text explains that background to Emergency We have already studied the changes that were taking place in Indian politics since 1967.

The text explains that indira Gandhi had emerged as a towering leader with tremendous popularity.

The text explains that this was also the period when party competition became bitter and polarised.

The text explains that this period also witnessed tensions in the relationship between the government and the judiciary.

The text explains that the Supreme Court found many initiatives of the government to be violative of the Constitution.

The text explains that the parties opposed to the Congress felt that politics was becoming too personalised and that governmental authority was being converted into personal authority.

The text explains that the Bangladesh crisis had put a heavy strain on India’s economy.

The text explains that after the war the U.S government stopped all aid to India.

The text explains that in order to reduce expenditure the government froze the salaries of its employees.

The text explains that this caused further dissatisfaction among government employees.

The text explains that credit: Abu PM says 6chapter the crisis of Democratic orDer

The text explains that these groups had taken to arms and insurgent techniques for the overthrow of the capitalist order and the established political system.

The text explains that known as the Marxist-Leninist (now Maoist) groups or Naxalites, they were particularly strong in West Bengal, where the State government took stringent measures to suppress them.

The text explains that under intense pressure from students, supported by the opposition political parties, assembly elections were held in Gujarat in June 1975.

The text explains that jayaprakash Narayan demanded the dismissal of the Congress government in Bihar and gave a call for total revolution in the social, economic and political spheres in order to establish what he considered to be true democracy.

The text explains that laxman in The Times of India, 16 April 1974 were organised in protest against the Bihar government.

The text explains that both the Gujarat and Bihar agitations were seen as anti-Congress and rather than opposing the State governments, they were seen as protests against the leadership of Indira Gandhi.

The text explains that do ‘committed judiciary’ and ‘committed bureaucracy’ mean that the judges and government officials should be loyal to the ruling party?

The text explains that of course, many people would be inconvenienced; but more than that, the economy of the country would come to a halt because goods are transported from one part to another by trains.

The text explains that it also raised issues like rights of the workers and whether employees of essential services should adopt measures like strikes.

The text explains that as the government refused to concede the demands of the striking workers, arrested many of their leaders and deployed the territorial army to protect railway tracks, the strike had to be called off after twenty days without any settlement.

The text explains that conflict with Judiciary This was also the period when the government and the ruling party had many differences with the judiciary.

The text explains that can the Parliament abridge Fundamental Rights?

The text explains that secondly, can the Parliament curtail the right to property by making an amendment?

The text explains that again, the Court said that Parliament cannot amend the Constitution in such a manner that rights are curtailed.

The text explains that thirdly, the Parliament amended the Constitution saying that it can abridge Fundamental Rights for giving effect to Directive Principles.

The text explains that this led to a crisis as far as the relations between the government and the judiciary were concerned.

The text explains that in this case, the Court gave a decision that there are some basic features of the Constitution and the Parliament cannot amend these features.

The text explains that but in 1973, the government set aside the seniority of three judges and appointed Justice A.

The text explains that the appointment became politically controversial because all the three judges who were superseded had given rulings against the stand of the government.

The text explains that thus, constitutional interpretations and political ideologies were getting mixed up rapidly.

The text explains that the petition, challenged the election of Indira Gandhi on the ground that she had used the services of government servants in her election campaign.

The text explains that jayaprakash announced a nationwide satyagraha for her resignation and asked the army, the police and government employees not to obey “illegal and immoral orders”.

The text explains that this too threatened to bring the activities of the government to a standstill.

The text explains that that is like asking the army to disobey the government!

The text explains that 98 Politics in India since Independence The response of the government was to declare a state of emergency.

The text explains that the 1977 elections for the first time saw the Opposition coming into power at the Centre.

The text explains that what would you consider as the reasons for this development?

The text explains that discuss the effects of Emergency on the following aspects of our polity. • Effects on civil liberties for citizens. • Impact on relationship between the Executive and Judiciary • Functioning of Mass Media • Working of the Police and Bureaucracy.

The text explains that in what way did the imposition of Emergency affect the party system in India?

The text explains that read the passage and answer the questions below: Indian democracy was never so close to a two-party system as it was during the 1977 elections.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Politics in India since Independence, Chapter 6: Rise of Popular Movements
